<sentence>The Prince of Wales' feathers, botanically known as *Polystichum aculeatum*, features a distinctive and elegant frond pattern.</sentence>
<sentence>Each frond, or leaf, is pinnately compound, meaning it is divided into numerous smaller leaflets called pinnae.</sentence>
<sentence>These pinnae are arranged alternately along the central stem, or rachis, of the frond.</sentence>
<sentence>The pinnae themselves are further divided into smaller segments, creating a finely textured appearance.</sentence>
<sentence>The overall shape of the frond is typically lanceolate, tapering to a point at the apex.</sentence>
<sentence>The edges of the pinnae are often sharply toothed or serrated, adding to the intricate detail.</sentence>
<sentence>The underside of the frond may bear sori, which are clusters of sporangia containing spores, often protected by a round, shield-like indusium.</sentence>
<sentence>The arrangement of these leaflets creates a graceful, arching effect, reminiscent of flowing feathers.</sentence>
<sentence>The texture is generally leathery and somewhat stiff, contributing to its robust appearance.</sentence>
<sentence>This detailed, layered structure gives the Prince of Wales' feathers its characteristic name and visual appeal.</sentence>
